## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Andrew is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history of depression, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension who presents with an upper respiratory infection. [6]  
Over the last week or so after doing yard work/landscaping, he developed fatigue and shortness of breath and feels he caught a cold that has worsened. [7]  
His shortness of breath is exertional (noted with lifting/carrying heavy bags of soil) and he became very winded. [12][13]  
He is not coughing up anything yet but feels like that may be next. [14][15]  
He felt a little warm but thought it was due to exertion. [16][17]  
He reports elbow pain “quite a bit” and knee tiredness/tension that he attributes to lifting bags. [18][19]  
He reports having received both COVID vaccines. [8][9]  
He denies any history of seasonal allergies. [10][11]  
Regarding depression, he reports doing well overall and has been using barre classes to relax, and he prefers to defer medication/psychotherapy for now and recheck in a few months. [20][21][56][58][60][61][62]  
For diabetes, he monitors sugars during the work week but often forgets on weekends; diet is mostly good except for some house parties, and he has not noticed elevated sugars during this illness. [22][23][24][25]  
For hypertension, he monitors BP more regularly during the work week than on weekends, reports it has been under control, and confirms he is taking his medication. [26][27][28][29]  
He denies nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. [30][31][32][33]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital signs in the office were normal and he was afebrile. [34][36]  
Cardiac exam: heart sounds “nice and strong.” [38]  
Pulmonary exam: scattered rhonchi bilaterally that clear with cough. [38]  
Extremities: mild lower extremity edema. [38]  
MSK: pain to palpation of both elbows. [38]  

## RESULTS
Chest X-ray was normal with no airspace disease and no pneumonia. [40][41]  
Diabetic labs showed hemoglobin A1c 8.0 (elevated), with goal discussed of ~6–7. [43][44][46]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
1) **Upper respiratory infection / suspected viral syndrome**: Clinician felt presentation was consistent with viral syndrome and expected improvement over a couple of days. [50][52]  
- Send COVID test to rule out COVID. [50]  
- Symptomatic treatment: Robitussin for cough; ibuprofen and/or Tylenol for fever. [54]  
- Return precautions: patient to notify clinic if symptoms worsen. [77]  

2) **Depression (stable by report)**: Patient reports doing fairly well and prefers to defer meds/psychotherapy for now. [21][56][60][61][62]  
- Offered medication regimen and/or psychotherapy referral if needed in the future. [56][58]  

3) **Type 2 diabetes mellitus (suboptimally controlled, A1c 8.0)**: Increase metformin to 1000 mg twice daily. [44][63]  
- Recheck hemoglobin A1c in 4 months (ordered). [65][67]  

4) **Hypertension (reported controlled)**: Continue lisinopril 20 mg daily. [68]  
- Refill provided/ordered per patient request. [72][73][74]  
- Lipid panel ordered. [70]  

5) **Follow-up**: Follow up visit planned in about 4 months. [75][76]

## HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Betty presents with poorly controlled asthma symptoms and reports frustration that she “can never catch a break,” with frequent coughing and wheezing. [1]  
She reports asthma flares are most commonly triggered when she gets sick with a cold, and she also had a flare after visiting her aunt’s house that she attributes to cat exposure, which she now avoids. [3]  
She reports breathing problems occurring about every month or every other month for the past six months, often requiring urgent care or primary care visits, and one episode was severe enough to require an emergency department visit. [5]  
Current asthma medications include two inhalers, but she primarily uses a “blue” inhaler identified as Ventolin (albuterol), and she feels the other inhaler “doesn’t work.” [7][9]  
She uses albuterol almost daily for cough/wheeze and also wakes a couple times per week at night needing to use it, with relief after use (typically two puffs) allowing her to return to sleep. [11][13][15][17]  
She reports only one asthma-related hospitalization in childhood (around age 8) and none since. [19]  
She reports several urgent care/ED-type visits in the past year that “almost always” result in prednisone, either as a short high-dose course (e.g., 5 days) or a taper over ~9–10 days. [21][23][25]  
Her last asthma exacerbation was about one month ago. [29]  
She denies seasonal allergies. [31]  
She denies cigarette smoking, marijuana use, and vaping/e-cigarette use. [35][36][37]  
She notes that secondhand smoke exposure at bars can irritate her breathing, so she tries not to go often. [39][41]  
She enjoys hiking but has to be careful due to coughing and wheezing and is looking forward to improving so she can hike again. [33]  

## PHYSICAL EXAM
Vitals: BP 128/82, RR 16, SpO₂ 99% on room air. [44]  
Cardiac: Regular rate and rhythm without rubs, murmurs, or gallops. [44]  
Lungs: Diminished breath sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally. [44]  
Neck: Supple, trachea midline, no lymphadenopathy, and no carotid bruit. [44]  
Extremities: Strong bilateral pulses, no digital clubbing, and brisk capillary refill. [44]  

## RESULTS
Pulmonary function testing performed today was consistent with asthma. [44]  

## ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Asthma, assessed as moderate intermittent, with frequent symptoms and frequent rescue inhaler use. [44][47]  
Start Flovent 110 mcg, 1 puff twice daily, with emphasis on consistent use of the inhaled steroid for long-term control and reduction in albuterol use. [47][44]  
Continue Ventolin (albuterol) as rescue therapy: 2 puffs every 4–6 hours as needed, and for acute symptoms use 1–2 puffs, wait to assess breathing, then repeat 1–2 puffs if needed. [47][44]  
Create an asthma action plan to help identify/manage triggers (e.g., cats, dust) and guide symptom-based management. [44][47]  
Follow up in 30 days/1 month, or sooner for worsening symptoms; patient agrees with the plan. [44][47][48][49]

Kyle presents after an incidental lung nodule was found during evaluation of a cough that his PCP diagnosed as bronchitis. [0][1]

A chest X-ray was obtained to rule out pneumonia, which identified a lung nodule, and a subsequent CT scan confirmed the finding, prompting referral to this clinic. [1]  
He is very nervous/scared about the diagnosis and evaluation. [1][27]  
He exercises regularly and does a lot of biking. [5][7][9]  
He has rheumatoid arthritis that is “fair”/stable on methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, with symptoms mainly in the hands and no recent flares. [12][13][15]  
He is a former smoker who quit about 7 years ago. [16][17][19]  
He denies pain and shortness of breath. [20][21]  
Family history is negative for lung cancer; mother had breast cancer and is doing well now. [22][23]

Vital signs: BP 124/76, HR 70, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, afebrile. [26]

## RESULTS

CT also shows no evidence of emphysematous disease. [26]

**1) Incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule (2 cm, smooth).** [26]  
Order pulmonary function tests (PFTs) to establish baseline lung function. [26]  
Schedule PET-CT to assess whether the nodule is metabolically active (which could suggest cancer or inflammation). [26]  
Recommend surgical removal via video-assisted thoracoscopy (three small right chest incisions) with resection of the nodule and a small portion of lung under general anesthesia (estimated ~1.5 hours). [26]  
Post-op plan discussed: admit after procedure with chest tube until the following day (anticipated bedside removal), likely 1-night hospital stay with discharge the next day. [26]  
Rationale discussed: although the nodule could be benign, given his smoking history, removal is recommended. [26]  

**2) Rheumatoid arthritis.** [12][26]  
Continue current medication therapy and follow up with his rheumatologist. [13][26]  

**3) Anxiety/fear related to workup and treatment plan.** [1][27]  
Provided reassurance that the care team will support him throughout the process. [2][28]
